markdown.markdown:
ECN 100B - Intermediate Microeconomics

Spring 2025

Prof. Muehlegger



# Announcements
- Sections will begin this week.
- Office hours will start this week as well. The dates and times are posted as a Canvas discussion item.
- You can also find the syllabus on Canvas.

 
# Class Outline
- Introduction and a few course details
- Review of Profit Maximization and Perfect Competition
- Questions

 
# I. Introduction and course details {.center}

 
# Introduction
- My contact information:
  - Office: 1120 SS&H
  - Office Hours: Thursday, 11 - 1pm, in person
  - Email: emuehlegger@ucdavis.edu
  - Twitter: @erichmuehlegger
  - My Other Day Job: Economist studying environmental regulation / energy policy.

 
# Teaching Team
- We have four great TAs who'll be part of the teaching team for the course.
  - Francisco Pizzi
  - Jessica Lyu
  - Maria Pulido
  - Robert Reaser
- You can find the office hour schedule on Canvas.
- You should feel free to attend any TA office hours.

 
# Course resources
- Lectures are the primary source of material for the course and all exams.
  - I provide handouts for lectures. I post them prior to class so you can take notes on them if you'd like. You can find them in Canvas -> Files -> Lecture Slides.
- Recommended Textbook
  - Microeconomics: Theory and Applications with Calculus, by Jeffrey M. Perloff
  - Available through Open Access, any edition is fine.
  - You can find it under "Bookshelf" on Canvas

 
# Classroom engagement and clickers {.small-content}
- This course will use "in-class" polling.
  - It helps keep you engaged in the material
  - It lets you know whether or not you understand the material
  - It give me real-time feedback on whether I've presented the material clearly
  - It facilitates class discussion
- Clicker questions will not be graded, nor will participation.
- We will use iClicker which is free for UC Davis students.
  - 9 - 10:20 (100B - A) https://join.iclicker.com/HTCC
  - 1:40 - 3:00 (100B - B) https://join.iclicker.com/MZWB

 
# Canvas
- I will disseminate material for the course through Canvas
- This includes:
  - Lecture Handouts
  - Problem Sets and Solutions
  - Questions to previous midterm and final
- Check Canvas often!
- Set your preferences to receive announcements by email!

 
# Course Grading (details in syllabus)
- Final Exam (35%)
  - 100B - A June 10th @ 8am.
  - 100B - B June 9th @ 8am.
- Midterm Exam (25%)
  - In class, Tuesday, May 6th.
- Problem sets (20%)
- Weekly Quizzes (20%)

 
# II. Profit maximization (Perloff 8.2)

 
# Firms seek to maximize profits {.smaller-content}
- We assume that the firm's objective is to maximize profits, subject to a variable the firm controls (often quantity).

$$
\max_q \ \Pi(q) = TR(q) - TC(q)
$$

- Taking the derivative and setting equal to zero.

$$
\frac{d\Pi(q)}{dq} = \frac{dTR(q)}{dq} - \frac{dTC(q)}{dq} = MR(q) - MC(q) = 0
$$

- Rearranging, we get the condition for firm profit maximization

$$
MR(q) = MC(q)
$$

- What is the intuition here?

 
# II. Perfect competition in the short run (Perloff 8.3)

 
# What are the assumptions of perfect competition?
- There are many, many firms
- Firms sell identical products
- All firms have full information about price and product characteristics
- There are no transaction costs
- Entry and exit from the market is free
- These assumptions have two important implications.
  - Firms are price takers. They can sell as much as they want without affecting the market price. This means that firms face a flat marginal revenue curve: $MR = P$.
  - In the long-run, entry and exit lead economic profits to be zero.

 
# Short-run competitive equilibrium
- The competitive equilibrium is the price and quantity where market supply equals market demand.
- We can find the competitive equilibrium in three steps:
  - Calculate the firm supply curve, how much each firm will produce as a function of price.
  - Sum the firm supply curves horizontally to get the market supply curve, how much all firms will produce as a function of price
  - Equate market supply and market demand.

 
# We'll consider a specific example
- In this example, we assume the following:
  - Market demand is $Q = 600 - 50P$
  - The market is perfectly competitive
  - There are 100 identical firms in the market
  - Each firm has identical costs: $TC(q) = 9 + q^2$
- Question: What can we learn about firm costs just from looking at the equation for TC?

 
# Step 1: What is the firm supply curve?
- To find the firm supply curve, maximize profits wrt to $q$.

$$
\max_q \ \Pi(q) = TR(q) - TC(q) = Pq - (9 + q^2)
$$

$$
\frac{d\Pi}{dq} = P - 2q = 0 \rightarrow P = 2q \rightarrow q^* = \frac{P}{2}
$$

- There are two things to note here.
  - The firm is maximizing profits, because $MR = MC$.
  - Since the market is perfectly competitive, $MR = P$.

 
# Profit maximization graphically
- Using an example with $P = 10$, and our firm supply curve, $q^* = \frac{P}{2}$.

$$
\Pi(q) = TR(q) - TC(q) = 10q - (9 + q^2)
$$

![FIGURE: profit-maximization graph with MR, MC, q*, and profit area](images/Lect1_Slide19.png){width=8in}

 
# Step 2: What is the market supply curve?
- To find the market supply curve, we sum the firm supply curves horizontally.
- Mathematically, with 100 firms
  - Firm supply curve: $q^* = \frac{P}{2}$
  - Market supply curve: $Q = 100q^* = 50P$
- Since we graph supply and demand with price on the vertical axis, we calculate the inverse market supply curve by putting price on the left-hand side.
  - Inverse market supply curve: $P = \frac{Q}{50}$

 
# Step 2: What is the market supply curve?
- Graphically, we horizontally sum the 100 firm supply curves to get the market supply curve.

!FIGURE: horizontal summation from individual firm supply to market supply; source=pptx; slide=21; bbox=unknown

 
# Step 3: Find the competitive equilibrium
- We calculate the competitive equilibrium by equating market supply and demand.
  - Market supply curve: $Q^S = 50P$
  - Market demand curve: $Q^D = 600 - 50P$
- Setting $Q^S = Q^D$, we have

$$
50P = 600 - 50P \rightarrow P = 6,\ Q^S = Q^D = 300
$$

- Since the firm supply curve is $q^* = \frac{P}{2}$, each firm produces 3 units.
- Alternatively, since $Q = 300$, and $N = 100$, $q^* = 3$.

 
# Graphical depiction of the competitive equilibrium in our example
- Since we graph with price on the vertical axis, we always want to have price on the left-hand side.

!FIGURE: firm and market equilibrium graphs with inverse supply and inverse demand; source=pptx; slide=23; bbox=unknown

 
# III. Perfect competition in the long-run (Perloff 8.4)

 
# In our example, do the firms earn economic profits?
- In this example, the competitive equilibrium is $P = 6,\ q = 3$.

$$
\Pi = TR - TC
$$

$$
= Pq - (9 + q^2) = 18 - (9 + 9) = 0
$$

!FIGURE: firm/market diagram showing zero economic profit at equilibrium; source=pptx; slide=25; bbox=unknown

 
# Economic profits
- In the example above, firms earn zero economic profits.
- What does this mean?
  - Economic profits include the opportunity cost of capital.
  - If a firm earns zero economic profits, their return in this industry is as good as the return they could receive from an equally risky industry.
  - Consequently, firms do not want to enter nor exit the market.

 
# Can firms ever earn economic profits?
- In the short-run, firms can change quantities, but can't enter or exit the market.
  - Thus, they can make short-run economic profits or losses.
- But, in a competitive market in the long-run, firms can freely entry and exit the market.
  - If firms are earning economic profits in the short-run, entry will shift the market supply outwards
  - If firms are bearing economic losses in the short-run, exit will shift the market supply curve inwards
  - Entry and exit will happen until economic profits are zero.

 
# Example with an inward demand shock
- Consider restaurants at the start of COVID - let's assume that they earn zero economic profit initially.
  - COVID shifts demand for restaurants inward -> Price falls
  - As equilibrium price falls, firms bear economic loses in short-run.
  - Firms exit the market, supply shifts inward until profit is zero again.

!FIGURE: individual-firm and market diagrams under inward demand shock (D to D', S to S'); source=pptx; slide=28; bbox=unknown

 
# IV. Perfect competition and welfare

 
# Social Welfare and Perfect Competition
- Consumer surplus is the amount, in dollars, that consumers benefit from the existence of the market.
  - Graphically, CS is the area below the inverse demand curve, but above price.
- Producer surplus is the amount, in dollars, that producers benefit from the existence of the market.
  - Graphically, PS is the area above the inverse supply curve, but below price.

 
# Consumer and Producer Surplus
- In our example from class
- Consumer surplus

$$
CS = \frac{1}{2} \times 300 \times (12 - 6) = \$900
$$

- Producer surplus

$$
PS = \frac{1}{2} \times 300 \times (6 - 0) = \$900
$$

- Is there any deadweight loss in the diagram?

!FIGURE: market graph showing CS and PS areas at Q=300; source=pptx; slide=31; bbox=unknown

 
# Market Failures and Welfare
- The perfectly competitive market maximizes welfare.
  - There is no deadweight loss and in the long-run, firms produce at the minimum of their average cost curves.
- But, there are three common sources of market failure, situations in which an unregulated market may not maximize welfare.
  - Firms are not price-takers (e.g., monopoly)
  - Actions create benefits or impose costs on others (e.g., air pollution)
  - Some parties have information that others don't (e.g., used car reliability)
- This is the where economics brings real value to policy and is an outline for this course!

 
# Major takeaways
- Perfectly competitive firms set $MR = MC$ to maximize profits.
- Price-taking means that $MR = P$. A firm can sell as much as it wants at price $P$ without affecting the market price.
- Horizontally adding the firm supply curves together gives the market supply curve.
- In the short-run, firms in a perfectly competitive market can earn economic profits or bear economic losses. But, in the long-run, free entry and exit will drive economic profits to zero.
- The perfectly competitive market maximizes welfare and doesn't create deadweight loss.
- But, if firms are not price takers, actions create externalities, or information is asymmetric, deadweight loss might exist in the unregulated market.

 
# Action Items
- Review the syllabus on Canvas
- Register for the course on iClicker Student
- Handouts are available before lectures. You can find them on Canvas.
- The textbook is available under Bookshelf on Canvas. Next class we will cover Perloff 11.1, 11.2.

$$
